I'm sorry, I'm naive on some points here. What does " reverse surge" mean to us in the bay area? When the water returns, does it happen like a surge?

it pulls the water out of the bay but when it comes back in, it looks like when the time comes in on any normal day. It happened several years back. The coolest thing I've ever seen. People were literally walking on the seabed of Tampa Bay.

the water will be sucked out temporarily. It’s happened here before. And we will see the sea floor for a ways out. The smell is awful, hoping we don’t have more stranded manatees like we did the last time it did it!

Reverse storm surge occurs when a storm makes landfall in such a way that significant winds blow offshore. Basically drying out the land. If you look up Hurricane Irma you'll be able to see what happens.
reverse surge is when the storm pulls the water out instead of pushing it in. Then as the storm passes the water returns.
Unfortunately the reverse surge can cause seawall damage due to not having pressure on both sides of the wall. We saw that in our area from Hurricane Irma.

my understanding is that rather than the storm pushing water into the bay as it approaches land...the wind actually shifts from the East and will push the water out of the bay to a certain extent. It means less surge for the bay hopefully.
looks like this, happened during Irma. You could walk all the way out into Tampa Bay (Hillsborough Bay). That’s Bayshore.
all the water gets sucked out instead of more being rushed in causing major flooding. You'll see the bay looks super empty. Really eerie!

Just a bit of comic relief...amongst all this uncertainty and scare. Thought you might enjoy this from my daughter -If you know me well, you know I have a weird thing about changing lyrics to fit life. Here is today's version....
Twas the night before Milton and all through the town
All the creatures were stirring, and running around
The shutters were hung on the windows with care,
Though hope was that Milton would not soon would be there;
The children were nestled all snug in their beds,
While visions of school closings danced in their heads;
I’m logging on Facebook, staying up late
Waiting for the latest Denis Phillips update.
When, what to my wondering eyes should appear,
But the whipping of wind, barely slowed by the sheer.
With storm surge and pressure, and lighting built-in,
I knew in a moment it must be Milton.
More rapid than eagles this hurricane came,
It wibbled, and wobbled, calling counties by name;
“Hernado or Pasco? Sarasota, Manatee?
Pinellas or Charlotte or maybe Lee?
To the top of the porch! to the top of the wall!
I’ll dash away! dash away! dash away all!”
He had high speed winds and a little eyewall.
That blasted on shore, like the start of a brawl.
He was scary and loud, coming in off the shelf,
But I laughed when I saw him, in spite of myself;
We’ve been working together and planning ahead
Being hurricane prepped, we had nothing to dread;
As he flew across Florida, his winds they did whistle,
Evacuated and sheltered, we avoided his missile.
But I heard him exclaim, going back to the heaven,
“You didn’t panic at all but remembered Rule#7!”
Rule #7:
